summary: Gruber highlights Max Krieger's Twitter threads on design — particularly one about Sony's Metreon complex in San Francisco — as an example of the thread format done well. He notes that Krieger is a natural at breaking design analysis into tweet-sized chunks with photos, and used the threads to promote a Kickstarter game. Gruber's search for another Krieger thread led him to discover Thread Reader, a site that collects Twitter threads onto single web pages. The post is a brief appreciation of how Twitter threads, when crafted well, can be genuinely engaging content, and how the web ecosystem still surfaces delightful discoveries.
key_points: Twitter threads can be annoying but when done well — with tweet-sized insights and inline photos — they become genuinely engaging, as Krieger demonstrates
Krieger cleverly used his design threads to promote his Kickstarter puzzle game, which Gruber backed as a thank-you for the content
Gruber discovered Thread Reader, a site that collects Twitter threads into single readable web pages, through a simple search
The post celebrates the serendipity of web discovery — one thread leading to a search leading to a useful new tool
money_quotes: Twitter threads can be annoying at times, compared to reading a regular old article. But when done well, they're engaging.
Krieger is a natural at the format — breaking all his thoughts into tweet-sized chunks and including plenty of photos illustrating his points.
I backed it simply to thank Krieger for these terrific design threads.
Thread Reader does just what you think it does: it collects Twitter threads on a single web page. It's exactly what I wanted.
Twitter and the good old fashioned World Wide Web can still be great.
key_insight: Twitter threads, when crafted with care and paired with the open web's discovery tools, can produce genuinely compelling design writing that rivals traditional articles.
